preamble: The reduction of a final oral stop [p] to a glottal stop [ʔ] is a common process in the development of 'Checked' or Rù sheng tonal categories. This Laryngeal Coda typically restricts the vowel's Pitch Contour to a short, often high-pitched or clipped profile, which is phonemicized as a distinct tone upon the loss or merger of the oral stop.
phoneticEffects: Debuccalization, Coda Reduction, Tonogenesis, Pitch Control, Checked Tone

examples.note: Final stops in Mon-Khmer provided the phonetic basis for the 'Nặng' and 'Sắc' tones in checked syllables in Vietnamese.
The 'Entering' tone (Rù sheng) in Middle Chinese resulted from syllables with final stops [p, t, k], many of which debuccalized to [ʔ] in modern dialects.
A stage in the development of the Rù (入) tone as syllables lost their original oral codas.
Thai maintains the 'checked' syllable distinction where final oral stops (or their glottalized reflexes) dictate the Pitch Contour and tonal register.
Simplification of final labial stops to a glottal coda in some dialects.

sources: Haudricourt, A. G. (1954). De l'origine des tons en viêtnamien.
Baxter, W. H. (1992). A Handbook of Old Chinese Phonology.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
